title: Light Editor UI — click-to-place animated lights on sprites (ImGui)
description:
In-engine ImGui editor for authoring the light-spot JSON sidecar used by np-0kv.3. Toggled by hotkey (F2 or a button in the existing debug panel). Lets Mike hand-author precise lights on a sprite instead of relying on AI-generated overlays (which come out misaligned).

UI layout (single ImGui window, docked or floating):

  +--------------------------------------------------+
  | Sprite: [mining_base ▾]  [+ add sprite…]         |
  +--------------------------------------------------+
  | +----------------+  | Lights (12)  [+ add]       |
  | |                |  | ┌────────────────────┐     |
  | | 512x512 sprite |  | │ ● (255,220,60) pulse│ <─ |
  | |  preview at    |  | │ ● (255,30,30) strobe│    |
  | |  1:1 or 2:1    |  | │ ● (60,180,255) stead│    |
  | |  zoom          |  | └────────────────────┘     |
  | |  clicks place  |  |                            |
  | |  lights here   |  | — Selected light ——————     |
  | |                |  |  u [0.55]  v [0.68]        |
  | |  live gizmos   |  |  color ██  size [8.0]      |
  | |  show each     |  |  kind [pulse ▾]            |
  | |  light as dot  |  |  hz [0.30]  phase [0.00]  |
  | |                |  |  [delete]                  |
  | +----------------+  |                            |
  |                     |  [save sidecar]            |
  +--------------------------------------------------+

Interactions:
  - Click-and-drag on empty preview area = place new light at that UV
  - Click an existing light gizmo = select it
  - Drag selected light gizmo = move it (update u,v)
  - Keyboard delete on selected = remove
  - Hotkey 'S' inside editor = save to <sprite>.lights.json
  - Hotkey 'F2' toggles editor on/off globally
  - Hotkey 'R' reloads from disk (discarding unsaved changes, with confirm)

Technical bits:
  - New file src/sprite_light_editor.{h,cpp} (~200-300 LOC)
  - Uses sokol_imgui which we already have
  - ImGui::Image of the sprite hull texture — sprite.h already exposes TextureSlot.view
  - Mouse-pos → UV: trivial from image screen rect
  - JSON save/load via existing src/json.cpp
  - Sidecar path: <sprite_base_path>.lights.json
    (e.g. assets/sprites/mining_base.lights.json)
  - Changes apply LIVE to the in-scene sprite so Mike sees the result
    without re-launch

Non-goals (YAGNI):
  - Multi-select
  - Copy/paste lights
  - Undo/redo (delete + re-add is fine for now)
  - Animated preview timeline scrubbing
acceptance_criteria: Pressing F2 opens the editor. Can select mining_base, click to place ~12 lights on hull features (radar dish, docking arms, processor drum windows), tweak per-light color/size/hz/kind/phase, save to mining_base.lights.json. Reopening the game loads that JSON and renders the same lights in-scene. No PNG lights layer needed.
notes: Shipped: in-engine ImGui Light Editor. sprite_light_editor.{h,cpp} (~340 LOC), F2 toggle, click-to-place on sprite preview with circle gizmos, drag-to-move selected, right-side color/size/hz/phase/kind editors, save/reload sidecar JSON (<sprite>.lights.json). Lights load automatically at sprite init if sidecar exists; fallback to hardcoded seed otherwise. Also fixed z-fighting flicker (clip-space depth bias of 0.001*w in sprite_spot.glsl — world-space biases don't work at far distances with the 1/500000 near/far nonlinear depth buffer).

title: Sprite renderer core (billboard quads with alpha + additive passes)
acceptance_criteria: Given a hull + lights PNG and a light spot list, sprite renderer puts a correctly-sized billboard into the Troy scene at a given world position. Lights flicker at configured frequencies. Alpha cutout clean (no dark fringes), additive lights feed into existing bloom pass.
notes: Shipped: sprite renderer core. Hull + lights two-pass billboard (alpha-blend + additive). ~250 LOC sprite.cpp, 60 LOC sprite.h, 60 LOC sprite.glsl. Mining base A/B with 3D mining base in Troy — sprite visually dominant. Follow-ups: np-0kv.3 (animated lights + bloom halos), hand-align lights overlay, np-0kv.4 (JSON schema).

title: Sprite-with-lights rendering pipeline (Privateer/Homeworld aesthetic)
description:
Pivot: most objects (stations, ships) become camera-facing sprite billboards with additively-blended animated light overlays. Keeps the current 3D pipeline intact (planets + asteroids stay 3D) but shifts the STATION/SHIP visual identity to a layered 2D approach that:

  - plays to our strengths (we have a pixel-artist agent + bloom post pass)
  - looks genuinely cinematic at low content cost (FTL / Stellaris / OG Privateer vibes)
  - dramatically simplifies the content pipeline — no more OBJ/mtl/materials.json/sidecars per ship

Architecture sketch:
  struct SpriteObject {
    sg_view hull_tex;           // RGBA8, alpha-blend
    sg_view lights_tex;         // RGBA8, additive — painted lights
    std::vector<LightSpot> anim;// code-animated blink/pulse lights
    HMM_Vec3 position; float scale; float alpha;
  };
  struct LightSpot { float u, v; uint32 rgba; float size, blink_hz, phase; };

Render order: opaque 3D (asteroids, planets) → sprite hulls (alpha-blended, sorted back-to-front) → sprite lights (additive, no depth write) → existing post/bloom pass.

Billboarding: always face the camera, no bank/pitch. Keeps the look clean 2D, matches OG Privateer. Sprite quads' world size is  units across regardless of aspect (aspect comes from the texture).

Scope for this epic:
  - Stations (mining_base, pirate_base) → sprites
  - Ships (when we add them) → sprites
  - Keep 3D: planets (sphere.obj does the job), asteroids (parallax feels good), sun, skybox, atmospheres.

Dependency note: the pixel-artist agent is the natural content source. Placeholder art (cropped 3D renders + hand-coded light spots) unblocks the engine work if art is stuck.
acceptance_criteria: A mining base renders as a sprite in Troy. It has a hull + lights layer + animated blinking lights that pulse independently. Looks at least as good as the current 3D mining base at equivalent distances. 3D asteroid belt and planet unchanged. Ctrl+M debug panel / Ctrl+N nav cycle / autopilot all still work.
notes:
Epic complete. Full sprite-with-lights pipeline shipped:
  .1 Sprite renderer core (billboard alpha + additive passes)
  .2 Placeholder sprites (obsoleted — real pixel art delivered)
  .3 Animated light-spot system (strobe/pulse/steady with bloom halos)
  .4 placed_sprites JSON schema (data-driven placement)
  .5 In-engine ImGui Light Editor (click-to-place + sidecar JSON)

Delivered aesthetic: industrial Privateer/Homeworld pixel-art stations with live navigation lights, no vertex-level lighting needed. Full roundtrip: artist delivers PNG → editor authors lights → sidecar JSON → load at runtime. Technical gotchas buried in shader comments: alpha-discard for transparent-fragment depth-writes, clip-space depth bias for z-fight at far distances (world-space nudges die in the ULP floor at 25000u with 1/500000 near/far ratio).

title: Galaxy data model + on-demand system loading
description:
Introduce a top-level universe/galaxy descriptor:
  - assets/galaxy.json (or equivalent) lists all known systems with:
    - id ('troy', 'pyrenees', etc.)
    - display_name
    - galaxy_position [x, y] — coarse 2D for the jump map
    - sector ('Troy Sector', 'Clarke Sector', ...)
    - json_path (path to the per-system JSON we already load)

Each per-system JSON's jump_point navs gain a 'links_to' field referencing the id of the system they jump to, plus optionally the target nav id (for reciprocal arrival).

Game boot: load galaxy.json first, pick a starting system (cli --system still wins if passed), only parse that one system's full JSON. Other systems load lazily when the player jumps.

The cli_remote can gain a /galaxy GET that returns the current system id + the full graph for dev tooling.

API (in C++):
  Galaxy g = load_galaxy("assets/galaxy.json");
  StarSystem* current = g.load_system("troy");
  StarSystem* next    = g.load_system("pyrenees");  // lazy
acceptance_criteria: galaxy.json exists and lists at least 3 systems (Troy + 2 neighbours). Game boots into Troy as today. C++ Galaxy struct can lazy-load any listed system. Jump-point navs in troy.json have links_to pointing to real galaxy ids. 'bd ready' stays green.

title: Multi-system universe: galaxy of star systems connected by jump gates
description:
The original Privateer had ~60 star systems connected by a jump-gate graph. Right now we have ONE system (Troy) loaded at startup. This epic introduces:

  (1) A universe-level data model — a 'galaxy.json' (or similar) listing all available systems and the jump-gate edges between them.

  (2) Visual jump gates — the mesh/geometry that lives at a nav_point of kind=jump_point. Must be recognisable at a distance, identifiable up-close.

  (3) Jump-through mechanic — fly near a gate, trigger jump, transition to the linked system and arrive at the corresponding reciprocal gate.

  (4) Seeding — all the canonical Privateer systems populated as JSON, either hand-authored or extracted from Vega Strike's privateer_wcu assets.

  (5) Galaxy map UI — classic Privateer jump map showing every system as a node and every gate as an edge; selectable, routable.

Dependency: the jump_point kind on nav_points comes from np-opa.1 (nav-point data model), so that needs to land first.
acceptance_criteria: Player can be in any loaded system, cycle to a jump-point nav, autopilot to the gate, trigger a jump, and arrive in the linked system at the reciprocal gate. Galaxy map shows the full graph. Canonical Privateer systems seeded (or at least 5-10 initially). Save/load (future) can persist 'current system'.

title: Nav-point data model in StarSystem JSON schema
description:
Add a 'nav_points' array to the system JSON schema. Each entry needs:
  - id (e.g., 'nav2')
  - name (e.g., 'Achilles')
  - kind (sun|planet|base|asteroid_field|jump_point)
  - position [x, y, z]
  - optional: linked_mesh (obj path of a placed mesh this nav 'contains')
  - optional: description (one-liner for the navmap tooltip)

Load into a new C++ struct NavPoint in system_def.h; add std::vector<NavPoint> nav_points to StarSystem. Parse in system.cpp alongside the existing fields.
acceptance_criteria: system_def.h declares NavPoint; system.cpp parses nav_points array from JSON; troy.json is updated with canonical 7-nav layout. Game still boots, logs '[system] ... navs=7'.

title: Troy: nav-point-based system layout (~1M u wide, coplanar slice)
description:
Rework system scale so every star system is a roughly 2.5D slice in the XZ plane, centered on the sun, ~1M units across. Nav points (like original Privateer/Freelancer) are the navigation skeleton — each nav is a named location you fly to, containing planets, bases, asteroid fields, or jump points.

Troy's canonical layout (from Privateer OG navmap):
  - Sun at (0, 0, 0) — center of the navmap
  - 6-7 nav points in a roughly coplanar arrangement on the XZ plane
  - Nav 2 = Achilles (mining base, square icon)
  - Nav 6 = Helen (agricultural planet, circle icon)
  - Distance between nav points: 600k-1M units across the system

All meshes (mining_base, pirate_base, sphere/Helen) should be relocated from their current ad-hoc coordinates to sit exactly on the nav point their canonical location maps to. Placed-mesh position in troy.json becomes ONE consequence of this system-scale reshape.

This is a scale change + a navigation framework introduction in one go. Once done, the camera at Nav 6 should look basically like it does today (Helen fills the screen) but the act of getting there from spawn should feel like a real journey.
acceptance_criteria: troy.json has a 'nav_points' array with ~7 entries, each at a canonical position. The sun is at (0,0,0). Nav 2 ~ Achilles mining base position. Nav 6 ~ Helen planet position. All existing placed meshes are re-anchored to their corresponding nav point. Running the game shows roughly the current views when the camera is placed near each nav, but free-flight between them now takes real in-cruise travel time.

title: Sprite-object JSON schema (add to system JSON alongside placed_meshes)
acceptance_criteria: troy.json gains a placed_sprites entry for the mining base. System loader parses it, sprite renderer draws it. The 3D placed_meshes entry can be COMMENTED OUT cleanly to compare A/B.
notes: Shipped: placed_sprites JSON schema. New PlacedSpriteDef in system_def.h (sprite stem, position, length_meters). parse_sprite() in system_def.cpp matches the placed_meshes parser pattern. main.cpp now iterates system.placed_sprites at init, with an unordered_map<string, SpriteArt> cache so repeated stems share one GPU upload. Sidecar lights load automatically via sprite_light_editor::load_lights_sidecar. troy.json has one placed_sprites entry (mining_base at 4500,400,25500); other systems (gliese, deep_void, arden_reach) gracefully show sprites=0 with no crash. Loader log now reports sprite count alongside meshes.

title: Animated light-spot system (blinking, pulsing, color)
acceptance_criteria: 3-4 animated lights on the mining base sprite blink at distinct frequencies and phases. Red running lights STROBE at 1Hz, yellow windows PULSE at 0.3Hz, cyan data light STEADY. Visible bloom halo around each.
notes: Shipped: animated light-spot system. New LightSpot struct (uv, color, size, hz, phase, kind ∈ {Steady, Pulse, Strobe}). New sprite_spot.glsl with hot-core + soft-halo radial falloff (saturates LDR scene buffer for bloom amplification). Third draw pass in SpriteRenderer iterates per-sprite lights, CPU-side intensity function drives animation via time_sec. 10 test lights on mining_base (4 arm strobes phase-chased, antenna strobe, 3 window pulses, 2 cyan steadies). Glows feed into existing bloom pass, look like proper navigation lights. Validates need for editor UI (np-0kv.5) — eyeballed UVs are close but not precise enough.

title: Placeholder mining base sprites (screenshot-based, pre-pixel-artist)
description:
Until pixel-artist billing clears, generate placeholder sprites from screenshots:

  1. Fly camera to a clean 3/4 angle of the existing 3D mining_base, sun upper-left
  2. Capture at a distance that makes it fill ~200x200 of screen
  3. Crop to square, resize to 256x256
  4. Background-removal (black → alpha via simple luma threshold; can be done in Python/PIL)
  5. Hand-code lights.png as a small Python script that paints ~10 dots at known approximate UV positions

This gets us end-to-end validation of the sprite pipeline. Swap in real pixel art when the agent is unstuck without touching any engine code.
acceptance_criteria: assets/sprites/mining_base.png and assets/sprites/mining_base_lights.png exist at 256x256, alpha channel correct, lights overlay aligns reasonably with hull silhouette. Sprite renderer loads them and displays the base in Troy.
notes: Obsoleted — pixel-artist delivered real 512² art once billing was topped up.

title: Jump-through mechanic: approach gate → transition to linked system
description:
Gameplay flow:
  1. Player autopilots to (or flies to) a jump-point nav
  2. When within trigger distance (~500 u) and travelling below cruise speed, a 'Jump Ready' prompt appears
  3. Press J (or auto-engage on arrival?) to initiate jump
  4. Brief effect: camera flash + audio sting (deferred — just a quick fade for v0)
  5. Destination system is loaded (lazy, via the Galaxy loader)
  6. Player is placed at the reciprocal jump gate in the destination system, facing into the system
  7. Camera position / velocity reset sensibly

Reciprocal matching:
  - If target nav has a 'links_to: {system, nav}' pair, arrival is at that exact nav
  - Otherwise, arrive at the first jump_point nav in the destination system that links back to our origin
  - If no reciprocal exists, log a warning and arrive at (0,0,0) near the sun

Hostile-gate reuse: like autopilot (np-opa.3), jumping should be blocked if hostiles are too close — Privateer tradition.
acceptance_criteria: Flying within ~500 u of a gate and stopping triggers a Jump prompt. Press J → fade out → destination system loaded → player placed at reciprocal gate. Round-trip (Troy → Pyrenees → Troy) works and lands you back near the original gate. Jump refused if hostile stub ever returns true.

title: Jump gate visuals: ring/portal mesh at jump_point navs
description:
A jump_point nav needs a distinctive, readable visual. Classic Privateer look is a glowing ring/donut with a shimmering portal in the middle; Freelancer used a big rectangular gate.

Options:
  (a) Reuse an existing WCU jump-gate OBJ if privateer_wcu ships one — cheapest, most authentic
  (b) Author a simple procedural ring in code (torus geometry + emissive shader) — fewer moving parts
  (c) Defer the mesh, draw a billboarded icon + glow for now — placeholder

Start with (a) if a usable mesh exists (check tools/import_wcu_meshes.sh sources). If not, (b) — a torus with an animated emissive ring can look surprisingly good.

Placement: the nav-point loader (np-opa.1) should auto-place the gate mesh at every jump_point nav's position, facing... something sensible (maybe toward the sun? or toward the linked system's direction). Configurable per-nav if needed.
acceptance_criteria: Every jump_point nav has a visible gate mesh at its position. Gate is recognisable at cruise distance (>10k u) and identifiable up-close. Emissive / glow element draws attention. No manual per-nav mesh placement needed in JSON — derived from the nav's kind.

title: Autopilot-to-nav on 'A' key (hostile-gated, strafe moves to Q/E)
description:
With N now cycling the selected nav (see np-opa.2), pressing 'A' engages autopilot TOWARD the currently-selected nav. Classic Privateer muscle memory.

Behaviour when A is pressed:
  - If no nav selected → no-op, flash a 'no nav selected' warning in the HUD
  - If hostiles within threat range → refuse to engage, flash 'hostiles detected — cannot autopilot' (OG Privateer behaviour)
  - Otherwise → rotate camera smoothly toward the selected nav, engage cruise, keep accelerating until within arrival distance (~5000 u), then auto-drop to normal speed

Key remap (A was previously strafe-left):
  - Q = strafe left (new)
  - E = strafe right (new, currently D)
  - A = autopilot (new)
  - D is freed up; reserve for later (maybe 'dock' when at a base)

Hostile gate stub:
  - New tiny file threat.{h,cpp} with 'bool hostiles_near(HMM_Vec3 pos, float radius)' — for now ALWAYS returns false (we have no NPCs). When we add enemies later this function grows; autopilot's caller never changes.

Any manual flight input (W/S/Q/E/space/shift/mouse) immediately cancels autopilot.
acceptance_criteria: A key engages autopilot toward selected nav; no-ops gracefully when none selected or hostiles present (stub always false for now). Q/E strafe left/right (was A/D). Autopilot rotates smoothly, cruises, auto-drops within 5000 u. Any WASD-era manual input cancels autopilot. On-screen controls hint updated.

title: Cycle-nav with 'N' + 2D navmap overlay (press M)
description:
Two tightly-coupled pieces:

(1) Cycling: 'N' cycles the selected nav forward through the system's nav_points list (shift+N to go back). Selection state lives on the player/camera struct. Small HUD readout in the top-right corner shows 'Target: Nav 2 — Achilles (432km)' whenever a nav is selected.

(2) Map overlay: 'M' toggles a 2D top-down projection of the system over the 3D scene. Frozen overlay (game keeps running). Shows:
  - Red cross-hair axis lines (evokes OG Privateer look)
  - Sun at center (yellow dot)
  - Each nav as an icon based on kind: circle=planet, square=base, triangle=jump_point
  - Labels: 'Nav 2 — Achilles', etc.
  - Current player position as a small ship triangle
  - Selected nav highlighted (arrow keys cycle, Enter = set autopilot target)

Render can live in its own small file (navmap.{h,cpp}) so we don't bloat main.cpp or debug_panel.cpp. Use sdtx for labels and sgl for the icons/lines — no new shaders needed.

Key collision note: the Ctrl+M debug panel shortcut stays; plain 'M' is the navmap. Two distinct chords, no conflict.
acceptance_criteria: N cycles the selected nav through nav_points; Shift+N reverses. Top-right HUD shows 'Target: Nav X — Name (distance)' whenever one is selected. M toggles the 2D navmap overlay with sun + all navs drawn with kind-correct icons. Ctrl+M debug panel still works.

title: Alpha-blend pipeline for translucent submeshes (glass domes)
description: Material::alpha_blend is recorded by the OBJ loader and stored in the cache, but the renderer currently draws every submesh opaque. Glass domes on mining_base etc. read as solid grey.
acceptance_criteria: Second mesh-render pass (after opaque) sorts back-to-front, enables SRC_ALPHA/ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA blending and disables depth writes. Only submeshes whose Material.alpha_blend==true participate.

title: Galaxy map UI: graph overlay of systems + jump routes
description:
Companion to np-opa.2's in-system navmap. A whole new overlay at a different key (probably 'G' for galaxy?) showing:
  - Every known system as a labelled node, positioned by galaxy_position
  - Every jump-gate edge as a line between its endpoints
  - Current system highlighted
  - Optional: route plotter — click a target system, shortest-path Dijkstra highlights the chain of jumps needed
  - Sector boundaries / colouring for visual grouping (Troy sector vs Clarke sector etc.)

Can share rendering primitives with the in-system navmap (navmap.{h,cpp}) — just a different dataset. Keep the overlay code in one file if it stays under 600 LOC, split if it grows.
acceptance_criteria: Press G → galaxy map overlay appears. All seeded systems drawn as labelled nodes with jump edges between them. Current system visually distinct. Pressing G again closes. Does not interfere with navmap (M) or debug panel (Ctrl+M).

title: Seed canonical Privateer system roster (extract from WCU or hand-author)
description:
Original Privateer has roughly 60 systems across 6 sectors (Troy, Pyrenees, Capella, Castor, Pollux, Eden — plus Gemini fringe). Each has a known set of navs and jump connections.

Sources to mine:
  - ../privateer_wcu assets — Vega Strike port probably has system JSONs
  - The original game's CIC/system data (fan wikis)
  - RimRunner's Privateer Remake (if accessible)

Strategy:
  - Write a tools/import_wcu_systems.py that walks the WCU system files, extracts nav positions + jump links, and emits our format
  - Fall back to hand-authoring the first 5-10 systems for Troy's immediate neighbourhood if auto-extract is infeasible
  - Minimum viable for this epic: Troy + Pyrenees + New Detroit + 2 others, connected in a simple line or small cluster

This is mostly a data-entry task once the schema from np-opa.1 + Galaxy model from this epic's child #1 are solid.
acceptance_criteria: At least 5 systems exist as valid system JSONs (Troy + 4 others). galaxy.json lists them with jump-gate edges. Jumping through Troy's gates lands in real, distinct systems. At least one multi-hop path exists (A → B → C).

title: hide_ui flag on /screenshot endpoint
description: Now that the debug panel is hidden by default, only the sdtx HUD + controls hint remain overlaid. For clean screenshots it'd be nice to toggle those off in the screenshot request rather than needing a permanent keybind.
acceptance_criteria: POST /screenshot accepts {hide_ui: true}. When set, sdtx layer is not drawn for that frame only. No permanent state change.

title: /camera/look_at dev_remote endpoint
description: Currently /camera/set takes yaw+pitch, so to aim at a specific world-space target I have to do atan2 math by hand in shell. A look_at endpoint accepting (target_xyz, up?) would be much more ergonomic for screenshotting.
acceptance_criteria: POST /camera/look_at with JSON {target:[x,y,z], up?:[x,y,z]} computes yaw/pitch from the current camera position and the target point. Optional 'pos' field to also move the camera in the same call.

title: Binary texture cache (.npttex) for faster warm start
description: After Phase D mesh cache, PNG decode dominates mining_base warm-load (65ms). Could cache decompressed RGBA pixel data next to each PNG and skip stb_image. Trade-off: more disk. Probably YAGNI unless cold/warm start matters more.
acceptance_criteria: Optional .npttex sidecar next to each PNG, mtime-invalidated. Warm load of mining_base drops below 15ms. Guarded by a build flag or cmdline switch since the disk cost is nontrivial.

title: Procedural normal map generation for planet surfaces
description: The agricultural planet preset generates a procedural diffuse texture but ships it with no normal map — the sphere mesh gets the flat-normal 1x1 fallback. The Phase C TBN shader is wired up and ready; we just need to generate a matching bumpy normal map from the same noise field.
acceptance_criteria: planet_texture::generate also produces a normal map. Planet surface shows visible micro-relief at grazing sun angles. Works for every preset (agricultural/barren/etc) without per-preset special cases.

title: Import WCU shared textures path for un-localized materials
description: pirate_base has 2 submeshes (tex0_0/tex1_0) that reference textures living in the shared VS textures dir, not in units/hospital_base/. Import script currently only searches the unit-local dir, so these come through with empty diffuse slots.
acceptance_criteria: tools/import_wcu_meshes.sh also searches VS shared textures path when a material name isn't found locally. Re-importing pirate_base fills in D7/S0/G0/N0 → D7/SX/GX/NX appropriately.

title: Hand-written atmospheric-param live tuner in debug panel
description: atm_thickness / atm_color / atm_strength currently only tweakable by editing troy.json and restarting. Would be nice as sliders in the debug panel's per-planet section, mirroring what we already do for ships.
acceptance_criteria: meshes/planets/sphere.obj entry in the Ship Sizer panel exposes the three atm_* params when the placed mesh has a planet_preset. Copy-JSON button emits the atm_* block.
